extra.reasoning:
The property requires that in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false (never opted_out AND sent simultaneously). 

sent can be set true in two transitions: t2 (send_attempt when NOT opted_out — safe) and t16 (send_attempt when opted_out AND stage>=4 — directly sets sent=true while opted_out is true). So a violation occurs if stage can reach 4 while opted_out is true, then send_attempt fires.

Key facts: (1) Only t1 (optout) sets opted_out=true, and it never resets it; no other transition clears opted_out. (2) The stage-advancing transitions (t3 stage0->1 on key_0, t6 stage1->2 on key_0, t9 stage2->3 on key_1, t12 stage3->4 on key_2) have guards depending only on stage, not on opted_out. So opted_out can be set first and the stage can still be driven to 4.

Trace (initial: opted_out=false, sent=false, stage=0):
1. optout (t1): opted_out=true, sent=false, stage=0
2. key_0 (t3, stage==0): stage=1, sent=false
3. key_0 (t6, stage==1): stage=2, sent=false
4. key_1 (t9, stage==2): stage=3, sent=false
5. key_2 (t12, stage==3): stage=4, sent=false
6. send_attempt: opted_out=true. t15 guard (opted_out and stage<4) is false since stage=4; t16 guard (opted_out and stage>=4) is true -> sent:=true. Now opted_out=true AND sent=true.

This reachable state violates globally(opted_out implies not sent). The counterexample uses 6 events, within the 7-event bound.
